# Background on *k*‑mer sets {#sec-kmer-sets}

Spectrum-preserving string sets, introduced in @sec-intro-kmers, offer one compact way to represent a /kmer spectrum.
By itself, however, such a representation gives no direct way to test whether an arbitrary /kmer $x$ belongs to the spectrum.
Building an efficient *index* is therefore a necessary step in any /kmer-based pipeline, from read mapping and taxonomic classification to genome assembly.
Depending on the application, this index may be built from an (r)SPSS, directly from raw reads, or from a reference genome, all of which may contain repeated /kmers.

At the most basic level, a /kmer *set* supports a single operation: *membership*, testing whether a given /kmer $x$ belongs to the set.
Many applications require more than this and associate each /kmer with satellite information such as an abundance count or a color.
A /kmer *dictionary* extends a /kmer set with a *lookup* operation: given $x$, it returns a unique integer identifier $i \in [0, n)$ that serves as a key into any external array of satellite values, or $-1$ if $x \notin S$.
Both sets and dictionaries are commonly used in a *streaming* regime, where queries are issued for every /kmer extracted consecutively from a sequence.
This modality is prevalent enough that several structures are explicitly designed for it, favoring cache-efficient access over runs of consecutive /kmers rather than minimizing the cost of individual queries.

This chapter focuses on three approaches representative of the state of the art for exact /kmer set indexing, and a broader survey of the landscape can be found in [@survey-kmer-sets].
SSHash [@sshash;@sshash2] and LPHash [@lphash] use compact hashing over the /skmer structure of the input to achieve very fast queries at moderate space.
The SBWT [@sbwt] exploits the colexicographic ordering of /kmers in the de Bruijn graph to compress the index close to the navigational lower bound, at the cost of slower $O(k)$-time queries.
FMSI [@masked-superstring-kmer;@masked-superstring-set-ops] indexes a masked superstring with an FM-index, offering a different point on the space-time tradeoff.

## Structural properties of /kmer sets {#sec-kmer-struct}

### Path locality and /skmers

As established in @sec-skmers, consecutive /kmers in a sequence tend to share the same minimizer and can therefore be grouped into /skmers.
In the context of indexing, this grouping has two important consequences.
First, there are far fewer /skmers than /kmers: each /skmer spans on average $(w+1)/2$ /kmers, the inverse of the minimizer density, reducing the number of objects to be indexed by the same factor.
Second, consecutive /kmers within a /skmer are stored contiguously, so a streaming query traversing a sequence left to right will access the same /skmer repeatedly before moving to the next, yielding favorable cache behavior.
More broadly, the /skmer structure induces a partial order on /kmers that an index can preserve: /kmers within the same /skmer can be assigned consecutive identifiers, enabling fast satellite data access for streaming queries, a property formalized and exploited by LPHash [@lphash].

### Colexicographic clustering and the Wheeler property

A second structural property stems from sorting /kmers in *colexicographic* order, comparing them from right to left.
Under this ordering, /kmers sharing the same length-$(k-1)$ suffix are adjacent, since they only differ in their leftmost character.
This clustering is the defining property of *Wheeler graphs* [@wheeler-graphs]: the de Bruijn graph of a /kmer set is a Wheeler graph, meaning its nodes can be totally ordered such that edges labeled with the same character form contiguous intervals.
This property enables *backward search*: membership of a query /kmer $x$ can be tested by starting from the interval of all /kmers ending in the last character of $x$, then iteratively narrowing it by prepending characters of $x$ from right to left, each step intersecting with a contiguous range.
Crucially, the colexicographic position of a /kmer implicitly encodes its identity, so the structure requires no explicit storage of the $2k$-bit label of each /kmer, only small per-node annotations.
This is the root of the space efficiency of BWT-based approaches.
The BOSS structure [@boss] realizes these ideas for the edge-centric de Bruijn graph; the SBWT [@sbwt] extends them to the node-centric view.

## Hashing-based dictionaries {#sec-kmer-hash}

### Sparse and skew hashing: SSHash

A *minimal perfect hash function* (MPHF) for a set $\mathcal{X}$ of $n$ keys is a bijection $f: \mathcal{X} \to [0, n)$ mapping each key to a distinct integer with no gaps [@survey-mphf].
MPHFs can be constructed using close to $\log_2 e \approx 1.44$ bits per key in expectation [@bbhash;@pthash], far below the cost of storing the keys themselves.
Since $f$ assigns an arbitrary value in $[0, n)$ to any input outside $\mathcal{X}$, a structure relying on an MPHF must store the keys or compact fingerprints separately to verify membership.

SSHash [@sshash;@sshash2] takes an SPSS as input, identifies /skmer boundaries on the fly to record their offsets within the strings, and builds an MPHF over the set of minimizers to index those offsets, while retaining the SPSS strings as the underlying storage.
Rather than hashing /kmers directly, the MPHF operates on the much smaller set of minimizers.
This is the *sparse* component of the design: since each /skmer spans on average $(w+1)/2$ /kmers, the MPHF is built over a key set far smaller than the /kmer set.
The input strings are stored at 2 bits per base, alongside a prefix-sum array encoding bucket sizes and an offsets array pointing to /skmer positions within the strings.
@fig-sshash illustrates the resulting data structure layout.

:::{#fig-sshash}
![](../fig/intro/sshash)

Overview of the SSHash dictionary data structure, from @sshash.
The input strings are stored contiguously; minimizers are mapped to bucket identifiers via an MPHF; the Sizes and Offsets arrays locate each /skmer within the strings.
:::

The *skew* component addresses the non-uniformity of bucket sizes: even with a random hash function, a small fraction of minimizers appears in a disproportionately large number of /skmers, yielding highly skewed bucket sizes.
SSHash encodes bucket sizes using Elias-Fano [@elias1974;@fano1971], which compresses non-uniform integer sequences close to their information-theoretic minimum.
For $m \ge 17$, where over 90% of minimizers have a single /skmer [@sshash, table 1], a secondary *skew index* groups minimizers by bucket size and builds a separate MPHF per group, limiting the number of /skmers to inspect at query time.

To answer a lookup query for /kmer $x$, SSHash computes the minimizer of $x$, retrieves the corresponding bucket via the MPHF, and scans its /skmers until $x$ is found, returning its identifier or -1.
Streaming queries benefit from caching the last minimizer: consecutive /kmers sharing the same minimizer extend the previous match without recomputing the bucket, reducing the average number of memory accesses per query.
In practice, SSHash achieves around 7-10 bits per /kmer and answers individual queries in hundreds of nanoseconds [@sshash, table 3].

A subsequent refinement [@sshash2] improves query efficiency on two fronts.
First, rather than storing the start of each /skmer and scanning it, the refined index stores a single position per minimizer occurrence; combined with the offset of the minimizer within the query /kmer $x$, this is sufficient to recover the exact location of $x$ in the SPSS in constant time.
Second, a *tag* array classifies each minimizer as singleton, light, or heavy based on how many /skmers share it; for *singleton* minimizers, the unique position is encoded directly in the tag itself, so that a lookup requires only two cache misses: one to evaluate the MPHF and one to read the tag.
Since over 97% of minimizers are singletons for $k = 31$ and $m \geq 19$, this shortcut applies to the vast majority of queries.
@fig-sshash2 illustrates the two layouts side by side.

:::{#fig-sshash2}
![](../fig/intro/sshash2){width=70%}

Comparison of the original and refined SSHash data structures, from @sshash2.
The tag array classifies minimizers as singleton, light, or heavy; singleton minimizers have their position encoded directly in the tag, avoiding any further memory access.
:::

### Locality-preserving hashing: LPHash

## Succinct dictionaries via the spectral BWT {#sec-kmer-sbwt}

### The SBWT and subset rank

### Index variants and space-time tradeoffs

## Masked superstrings {#sec-kmer-ms}

## Comparison {#sec-kmer-comparison}
